title: Multimodal Retrieval Use Cases
concept: Searching a product catalog by photo, or documents by a mix of diagrams and captions.

exercises.prompt: In our last lesson, we looked at joint embedding models. How do they represent text and images?

options.text: They keep text and images in separate databases that cannot talk to each other.
They project both text and images into a single, shared vector space.
They discard the images and only look at user-generated hashtags.

exercises.explain: CLIP-style joint embeddings place both text and images into a single, shared coordinate system (vector space), allowing them to be compared directly.

cards.title: Search Beyond Words
cards.body: Have you ever tried to describe a highly specific pattern, like a vintage wallpaper design, using only text search? It's incredibly difficult. Multimodal retrieval changes this by letting you search using the patterns and images themselves.

cards.title: What is Multimodal Retrieval?
cards.body: Multimodal retrieval is the process of using one type of media (like a photo or sketch) to search for and retrieve another type of media (like a product page, PDF diagram, or text description) because they share a single, unified vector space.

cards.title: The Universal Catalog
cards.body: Think of multimodal retrieval like a universal library catalog cards index. Instead of separate catalogs for books, audiobooks, and photo albums, everything is filed under the exact same system where a photo of a sunset sits right next to a poem describing a sunset.

cards.title: Real-world Troubleshooting
cards.body: Imagine a repair technician taking a picture of a broken gear. The system instantly retrieves the exact pages of a PDF manual containing both that gear's CAD diagram and the text instructions for fixing it, using only the image as the query.

cards.title: The Translation Myth
cards.body: People think computers must first convert an image into text tags (like 'blue stripe shirt') to search for it. In reality, multimodal models compare the raw image embedding directly to text embeddings without any intermediate translation step.

cards.title: Visualizing Cross-Modal Search
cards.body: See how multimodal databases allow different formats to bridge the gap and interact directly in search.

left.text: User uploads a photo of a running shoe.

right.text: System retrieves the text product specs and reviews.

exercises.prompt: How can a search engine match a user's text query to a specific diagram inside a textbook PDF?

options.text: The system translates the PDF diagrams into text descriptions first.
Both the diagrams and their text captions are embedded into the same shared vector space.
The system uses basic file-name matching.
The vector database converts text queries into synthetic images.

exercises.explain: A joint text-image embedding space maps both formats to nearby coordinates based on their semantic meaning, allowing a visual query to match text documents directly.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a multimodal retrieval system, you can search a collection of images using a text query, even if the images have no text tags.
exercises.explain: Multimodal embedding spaces project text and images into the same dimensional space, allowing you to use an image as a query to find text, or vice versa.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term that describes searching across different types of media:
exercises.sentence: Using a photo query to find text documents is called cross-modal retrieval.
exercises.explain: Because images and text are mapped to the same coordinate space, we can perform cross-modal search, where a query of one type (image) finds a result of another type (text).

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes a real-world application of multimodal retrieval?

options.text: Searching a database of text articles by typing keywords into a search bar.
Finding a song by typing the artist's name and the song release year.
An app where a user uploads a photo of a plant to retrieve its scientific name and care instructions.

exercises.explain: Option C demonstrates a practical application of multimodal search where a camera image is used to query a catalog and retrieve matching physical products. Options A and B are traditional unimodal text searches.

exercises.prompt: You are building a shopping assistant app. A user takes a photo of a vintage lamp at a flea market and wants to find similar items in your inventory. What is the most efficient multimodal search workflow?

options.text: Convert the customer's photo into an embedding vector and run a similarity search directly against the catalog image vectors.
Force the customer to write a text description of the photo and use keyword search.
Only match the file size and date of the customer's photo against the database metadata.
Use a slow, expensive LLM to write 50 text tags for the user's photo before running a classic SQL database query.

exercises.explain: A multimodal vector database indexes both text and images in the same vector space. Because the database contains vectors representing the images directly, you can query it using a camera snapshot without first converting that image to text keywords.
